7: YHWH amar elai beni atta ani hayom yelidticha (Yhwh ʾāmar ʾēlay, bĕnî ʾattâ ʾănî hayyôm yĕlídtîkā): 'The LORD said to me: You are my Son; today I have begotten you.' The royal adoption formula of Ps 2:7 was applied to the Davidic king at his enthronement — the king became YHWH's 'son' in a representative capacity (2 Sam 7:14; Ps 89:26-27). The NT applies it to Jesus at three Christological moments: the baptism (Matt 3:17), the transfiguration (2 Pet 1:17), and the resurrection (Acts 13:33; Heb 1:5; 5:5). The resurrection is the decisive 'today' in NT usage: the day of Christ's enthronement at the Father's right hand is the day of divine sonship's full eschatological declaration.
1:
לָמָּה רָגְשׁוּ גוֹיִם (lāmmāh rāgĕshû gôyim) — "why do the nations rage?" The verb rāgash (to be in tumult, to throng/conspire) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible outside of this form; the LXX translates ἐφρύαξαν (to be insolent/neigh like horses). The word suggests uncontrolled, agitated motion — the nations in restless, futile commotion. Acts 4:25 cites this verse at the Jerusalem church's prayer after the arrest of Peter and John, identifying the "nations" with Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and the people of Israel.

רִיק (rîq, "in vain") — the peoples plot rîq: emptiness, futility. The futility is built into the conspiracy from the beginning; not that it fails but that it is empty of substance.
2: מְשִׁיחוֹ (mĕshîḥô, "his Anointed One") — from māshaḥ, to anoint with oil. The word appears here in the context of the Davidic king on whom the LORD's oil was poured. "His Anointed" is the one who belongs to the LORD by virtue of anointing — the divine claim on the person. The conspiracy is therefore not against a human king but against the one who has been set apart by God.
3: מוֹסְרוֹתֵימוֹ (môsĕrôtêmô, "their bonds") and עֲבֹתֵימוֹ (ʿăbōtêmô, "their cords") — the rebels' self-understanding: they experience divine sovereignty as constraint. The cords (ʿăbōt) can also mean "ropes" used to bind animals — they picture themselves as livestock under a yoke. The irony is that what they call bonds are the ordering structures that make flourishing possible.
4: יִשְׂחַק (yisḥāq, "laughs") — the divine response to the conspiracy is not panic but laughter. The same root as the name Isaac (yitsḥāq), from ṣāḥaq — to laugh, to play. The LORD who sits enthroned in the heavens finds the nations' plots incongruous with reality. יִלְעַג (yilʿag, "scoffs") — to mock, to stammer in imitation; the LORD's scoffing is not contemptuous anger but the amusement of absolute security.
5: בְּאַפּוֹ (bĕʾappô, "in his wrath") and בַחֲרוֹן אַפּוֹ (baḥărôn ʾappô, "in his burning anger") — ʾap literally means "nostril/nose"; anger in Hebrew is conceived as snorting/fuming breath. Ḥărôn (burning, from ḥārāh, to burn) intensifies it: not merely irritation but heated wrath. The divine speech that follows (v6) is the decree that settles the matter.
6: נָסַכְתִּי (nāsaktî, "I have installed") — the verb nāsak typically means to pour out (as a libation) or to cast metal; in this context it means "to set in place" as one sets a foundation stone. The perfect tense (nāsaktî) declares a completed act: the installation of the King is already done. Zion as הַר-קָדְשִׁי (har qodshî, "my holy hill") designates the place as consecrated territory belonging to the LORD.
8: שְׁאַל מִמֶּנִּי (shĕʾal mimmennî, "ask of me") — the imperative of shāʾal: the invitation to request. The Anointed One is invited to ask for his inheritance — the nations and the ends of the earth. The inheritance motif connects to the Abrahamic promise (Gen 17:6) and the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16). The gift is cosmic in scope: אַפְסֵי-אָרֶץ (ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ) — the "ends/limits of the earth," the outermost extremities.
9: תְּרֹעֵם בְּשֵׁבֶט בַּרְזֶל (tĕrōʿēm bĕshêbet barzel, "you shall rule them with an iron rod") — shêbet is both "rod/staff" (the shepherd's implement) and the scepter of authority. Iron makes it a weapon of force rather than a pastoral tool. תְּנַפְּצֵם (tĕnappĕṣêm, "shatter them") — piel of nāpaṣ: to smash into pieces, used of breaking pottery (Jer 19:11). This verse is cited three times in Revelation (2:27; 12:5; 19:15) as the authority given to the risen Christ and shared with those who overcome.
10: הַשְׂכִּילוּ (haśkîlû, "be wise") — hiphil imperative of śākal: not merely "be intelligent" but "act with insight/prudence" — wisdom that produces right action. The address turns from narrative to direct exhortation. הִוָּסְרוּ (hiwwāsĕrû, "take warning") — niphal of yāsar: receive instruction/discipline; the word is often used for parental or divine discipline.
11: עִבְדוּ אֶת-יְהוָה בְּיִרְאָה (ʿibdû ʾet-YHWH bĕyirʾāh, "serve the LORD with reverent fear") — ʿābad (serve/work) can refer to labor, worship, and service simultaneously; the kings are called to re-orient their service from self-appointed dominion to submission to the LORD. גִּילוּ בִּרְעָדָה (gîlû birʿādāh, "celebrate with trembling") — gîl is exultation, a word for intense, even physical, joy; rĕʿādāh is shaking, trembling. The pairing is paradoxical: joyful trembling, not joyless fear. Paul echoes this: "work out your salvation with fear and trembling" (Phil 2:12).
12: בַּר (bar, "Son") — an Aramaic word (not the Hebrew ben) appearing in this Hebrew poem; its rarity has made it a textual puzzle. The MT is supported by many manuscripts as bar ("son"), giving "pay homage to the Son." The LXX takes a different path (δράξασθε παιδείας, "embrace discipline"), suggesting the Greek translators read differently or interpreted the difficult term. The MKT follows MT. The psalm ends on the same note as it began: אַשְׁרֵי (ʾashrê) — "Blessed" — forming an inclusio with Psalm 1:1: the blessed life belongs to those who take refuge in the Son.
